text.NABRE: Moreover, the dues of five thousand silver shekels that used to be taken from the revenue of the sanctuary every year shall be canceled, since these funds belong to the priests who perform the services.
text.NRSV: Moreover, the five thousand shekels of silver that my officials have received every year from the income of the services of the temple, this too is cancelled, because it belongs to the priests who minister there.

text.DRB: And whosoever fly into the temple that is in Jerusalem, and in all the coasts thereof, in the king's danger for matter, let them be dismissed, & all things, that they have in my kingdom, let them have it free.
text.KJV: And whosoever they be that flee unto the temple at Jerusalem, or be within the liberties hereof, being indebted unto the king, or for any other matter, let them be at liberty, and all that they have in my realm.
text.NABRE: All who take refuge in the temple of Jerusalem or in any of its precincts, because of money they owe the king, or because of any other debt, shall be released, together with all the goods they possess in my kingdom.
text.NRSV: And all who take refuge at the temple in Jerusalem, or in any of its precincts, because they owe money to the king or are in debt, let them be released and receive back all their property in my kingdom.

text.DRB: And to build or repair the works of the holy places, the charges shall be given of the king's account:
text.KJV: For the building also and repairing of the works of the sanctuary expences shall be given of the king’s accounts.
text.NABRE: The cost of rebuilding and restoring the structures of the sanctuary shall be covered out of the royal revenue.
text.NRSV: ‘Let the cost of rebuilding and restoring the structures of the sanctuary be paid from the revenues of the king.

text.DRB: and to build the walls in Jerusalem, and to fortify them round about, the charges shall be given of the king's account, and to build the walls in Jurie.
text.KJV: Yea, and for the building of the walls of Jerusalem, and the fortifying thereof round about, expences shall be given out of the king’s accounts, as also for the building of the walls in Judea.
text.NABRE: Likewise the cost of building the walls of Jerusalem and fortifying it all around, and of building walls in Judea, shall be donated from the royal revenue.”
text.NRSV: And let the cost of rebuilding the walls of Jerusalem and fortifying it all round, and the cost of rebuilding the walls in Judea, also be paid from the revenues of the king.’

text.DRB: But as Jonathas, & the people heard these words, they believed them not, nor received them: because they remembered the great mischief, that he had done in Israel, and had afflicted them exceedingly.
text.KJV: Now when Jonathan and the people heard these words, they gave no credit unto them, nor received them, because they remembered the great evil that he had done in Israel; for he had afflicted them very sore.
text.NABRE: When Jonathan and the people heard these words, they neither believed nor accepted them, for they remembered the great evil that Demetrius had done in Israel, and the great tribulation he had brought upon them.
text.NRSV: Death of Demetrius When Jonathan and the people heard these words, they did not believe or accept them, because they remembered the great wrongs that Demetrius had done in Israel and how much he had oppressed them.

text.DRB: And their liking was toward Alexander, because he had been the first mover of the words of peace unto them, & him they aided all days.
text.KJV: But with Alexander they were well pleased, because he was the first that entreated of true peace with them, and they were confederate with him always.
text.NABRE: They therefore decided in favor of Alexander, for he had been the first to address them peaceably, and they remained his allies for the rest of his life.
text.NRSV: They favoured Alexander, because he had been the first to speak peaceable words to them, and they remained his allies all his days.

text.DRB: And king Alexander gathered a great army, & moved the camp near to Demetrius.
text.KJV: Then gathered king Alexander great forces, and camped over against Demetrius.
text.NABRE: Then King Alexander gathered together a large army and encamped opposite Demetrius.
text.NRSV: Now King Alexander assembled large forces and encamped opposite Demetrius.

text.DRB: And the two kings joined battle, and the army of Demetrius fled, & Alexander pursued him, & urged them sore.
text.KJV: And after the two kings had joined battle, Demetrius’ host fled: but Alexander followed after him, and prevailed against them.
text.NABRE: The two kings joined battle, and when the army of Demetrius fled, Alexander pursued him, and overpowered his soldiers.
text.NRSV: The two kings met in battle, and the army of Demetrius fled, and Alexander pursued him and defeated them.

text.DRB: And the battle grew very sore, till the sun went down: & Demetrius was slain in that day.
text.KJV: And he continued the battle very sore until the sun went down: and that day was Demetrius slain.
text.NABRE: He pressed the battle hard until sunset, and Demetrius fell that day.
text.NRSV: He pressed the battle strongly until the sun set, and on that day Demetrius fell.

text.DRB: And Alexander sent legates to Ptolomee king of Ægypt according to these words, saying:
text.KJV: Afterward Alexander sent ambassadors to Ptolemee king of Egypt with a message to this effect:
text.NABRE: Treaty of Ptolemy and Alexander. Alexander sent ambassadors to Ptolemy, king of Egypt, with this message:
text.NRSV: Treaty of Ptolemy and Alexander Then Alexander sent ambassadors to Ptolemy king of Egypt with the following message:

text.DRB: Because I am returned into my Kingdom, and am set in the seat of my fathers, & have obtained the princedom, & have destroyed Demetrius, & possessed our country,
text.KJV: Forasmuch as I am come again to my realm, and am set in the throne of my progenitors, and have gotten the dominion, and overthrown Demetrius, and recovered our country;
text.NABRE: “Now that I have returned to my realm, taken my seat on the throne of my ancestors, and established my rule by crushing Demetrius and gaining control of my country—
text.NRSV: ‘Since I have returned to my kingdom and have taken my seat on the throne of my ancestors, and established my rule—for I crushed Demetrius and gained control of our country;

text.DRB: and have joined battle with him, and he is destroyed himself, & his camp by us, and we sit in the seat of his kingdom:
text.KJV: For after I had joined battle with him, both he and his host was discomfited by us, so that we sit in the throne of his kingdom:
text.NABRE: for I engaged him in battle, he and his army were crushed by us, and we assumed his royal throne—
text.NRSV: I met him in battle, and he and his army were crushed by us, and we have taken our seat on the throne of his kingdom—

text.DRB: and now let us make amity one with another: & give me thy daughter to wife, and I will be thy son in law, & I will give thee gifts, and to her dignity.
text.KJV: Now therefore let us make a league of amity together, and give me now thy daughter to wife: and I will be thy son in law, and will give both thee and her as according to thy dignity.
text.NABRE: let us now establish friendship with each other. Give me now your daughter for my wife; and as your son-in-law, I will give to you and to her gifts worthy of you.”
text.NRSV: now therefore let us establish friendship with one another; give me now your daughter as my wife, and I will become your son-in-law, and will make gifts to you and to her in keeping with your position.’

text.DRB: And king Ptolomee answered, saying: Happy is the day wherein thou art returned to the land of thy fathers, and art set in the seat of their kingdom.
text.KJV: Then Ptolemee the king gave answer, saying, Happy be the day wherein thou didst return into the land of thy fathers, and satest in the throne of their kingdom.
text.NABRE: King Ptolemy answered in these words: “Happy the day on which you returned to the land of your ancestors and took your seat on their royal throne!
text.NRSV: Ptolemy the king replied and said, ‘Happy was the day on which you returned to the land of your ancestors and took your seat on the throne of their kingdom.

text.DRB: And now I will do to thee that which thou hast written: but meet me at Ptolomais, that we may see one another, and I may despouse her to thee as thou hast said.
text.KJV: And now will I do to thee, as thou hast written: meet me therefore at Ptolemais, that we may see one another; for I will marry my daughter to thee according to thy desire.
text.NABRE: I will do for you what you have written; but meet me in Ptolemais, so that we may see each other, and I will become your father-in-law as you have proposed.”
text.NRSV: And now I will do for you as you wrote, but meet me at Ptolemais, so that we may see one another, and I will become your father-in-law, as you have said.’

text.DRB: And Ptolomee went out of Ægypt, he & Cleopatra his daughter, & he came to Ptolomais in the year an hundred threescore and two.
text.KJV: So Ptolemee went out of Egypt with his daughter Cleopatra, and they came unto Ptolemais in the hundred threescore and second year:
text.NABRE: So Ptolemy with his daughter Cleopatra set out from Egypt and came to Ptolemais in the one hundred and sixty-second year.
text.NRSV: So Ptolemy set out from Egypt, he and his daughter Cleopatra, and came to Ptolemais in the one hundred and sixty-second year.

text.DRB: And Alexander the king met him, & he gave him Cleopatra his daughter: and he made her marriage at Ptolomais, as kings in great glory.
text.KJV: Where king Alexander meeting him, he gave unto him his daughter Cleopatra, and celebrated her marriage at Ptolemais with great glory, as the manner of kings is.
text.NABRE: There King Alexander met him, and Ptolemy gave him his daughter Cleopatra in marriage. Their wedding was celebrated at Ptolemais with great splendor according to the custom of kings.
text.NRSV: King Alexander met him, and Ptolemy gave him his daughter Cleopatra in marriage, and celebrated her wedding at Ptolemais with great pomp, as kings do.

text.DRB: And king Alexander wrote to Jonathas, that he should come to meet him.
text.KJV: Now king Alexander had written unto Jonathan, that he should come and meet him.
text.NABRE: King Alexander also wrote to Jonathan to come and meet him.
text.NRSV: Then King Alexander wrote to Jonathan to come and meet him.

text.DRB: And he went with glory to Ptolomais, & he met there the two kings, & he gave them much silver, & gold, & gifts: & he found grace in their sight.
text.KJV: Who thereupon went honourably to Ptolemais, where he met the two kings, and gave them and their friends silver and gold, and many presents, and found favour in their sight.
text.NABRE: So he went with pomp to Ptolemais, where he met the two kings and gave them and their friends silver and gold and many gifts and thus won their favor.
text.NRSV: So he went with pomp to Ptolemais and met the two kings; he gave them and their Friends silver and gold and many gifts, and found favour with them.

text.DRB: And there assembled against him pestilent men of Israel, wicked men soliciting against him: & the king attended not to them.
text.KJV: At that time certain pestilent fellows of Israel, men of a wicked life, assembled themselves against him, to accuse him: but the king would not hear them.
text.NABRE: Some villainous men of Israel, transgressors of the law, united against him to accuse him, but the king paid no heed to them.
text.NRSV: A group of malcontents from Israel, renegades, gathered together against him to accuse him; but the king paid no attention to them.

text.DRB: And he commanded Jonathas to be changed of his garments, & that he should be clothed with purple: and so they did. And the king placed him to sit with himself.
text.KJV: Yea more than that, the king commanded to take off his garments, and clothe him in purple: and they did so.
text.NABRE: The king ordered Jonathan to be divested of his garments and to be clothed in royal purple; and so it was done.
text.NRSV: The king gave orders to take off Jonathan’s garments and to clothe him in purple, and they did so.

text.DRB: And he said to his princes: Go ye out with him into the midst of the city, & proclaim, that no man solicit against him for any matter, & that no man be troublesome unto him for any cause.
text.KJV: And he made him sit by himself, and said into his princes, Go with him into the midst of the city, and make proclamation, that no man complain against him of any matter, and that no man trouble him for any manner of cause.
text.NABRE: The king also had him seated at his side. He said to his magistrates: “Go with him to the center of the city and make a proclamation that no one is to bring charges against him on any grounds or be troublesome to him for any reason.”
text.NRSV: The king also seated him at his side; and he said to his officers, ‘Go out with him into the middle of the city and proclaim that no one is to bring charges against him about any matter, and let no one annoy him for any reason.’

text.DRB: And it came to pass, as they that solicited saw his glory that was published, & him covered with purple, they all fled:
text.KJV: Now when his accusers saw that he was honored according to the proclamation, and clothed in purple, they fled all away.
text.NABRE: When his accusers saw the honor paid to him according to the king’s proclamation, and him clothed in purple, they all fled.
text.NRSV: When his accusers saw the honour that was paid him, in accordance with the proclamation, and saw him clothed in purple, they all fled.

text.DRB: & the king magnified him, & wrote him amongst his chief friends, & made him Duke, & partaker of the princedom.
text.KJV: So the king honoured him, and wrote him among his chief friends, and made him a duke, and partaker of his dominion.
text.NABRE: And so the king honored him, enrolling him among his Chief Friends, and he made him governor and chief of the province.
text.NRSV: Thus the king honoured him and enrolled him among his chief Friends, and made him general and governor of the province.

text.DRB: And Jonathas returned into Jerusalem with peace & joy.
text.KJV: Afterward Jonathan returned to Jerusalem with peace and gladness.
text.NABRE: So Jonathan returned in peace and happiness to Jerusalem.
text.NRSV: And Jonathan returned to Jerusalem in peace and gladness.

text.DRB: In the year an hundreth sixty five came Demetrius the son of Demetrius from Crete into the land of his fathers.
text.KJV: Furthermore in the; hundred threescore and fifth year came Demetrius son of Demetrius out of Crete into the land of his fathers:
text.NABRE: Jonathan Defeats Apollonius. In the one hundred and sixty-fifth year, Demetrius, son of Demetrius, came from Crete to the land of his ancestors.
text.NRSV: Apollonius Is Defeated by Jonathan In the one hundred and sixty-fifth year Demetrius son of Demetrius came from Crete to the land of his ancestors.

text.DRB: And Alexander the king heard it, & he was very sorry, & returned to Antioch.
text.KJV: Whereof when king Alexander heard tell, he was right sorry, and returned into Antioch.
text.NABRE: When King Alexander heard of it he was greatly troubled, and returned to Antioch.
text.NRSV: When King Alexander heard of it, he was greatly distressed and returned to Antioch.

text.DRB: And Demetrius the king made Apollonius general, who was ruler of Coelesiria: & he gathered a great army, & came to Jamnia: & he sent to Jonathas the high priest,
text.KJV: Then Demetrius made Apollonius the governor of Celosyria his general, who gathered together a great host, and camped in Jamnia, and sent unto Jonathan the high priest, saying,
text.NABRE: Demetrius set Apollonius over Coelesyria. Having gathered a large army, Apollonius encamped at Jamnia. From there he sent this message to Jonathan the high priest:
text.NRSV: And Demetrius appointed Apollonius the governor of Coelesyria, and he assembled a large force and encamped against Jamnia. Then he sent the following message to the high priest Jonathan:

text.DRB: saying: Thou only resistest us, & I am brought into derision, & into reproach, because thou doest exercise power against us in the mountains.
text.KJV: Thou alone liftest up thyself against us, and I am laughed to scorn for thy sake, and reproached: and why dost thou vaunt thy power against us in the mountains?
text.NABRE: “You are the only one who resists us. I am laughed at and put to shame on your account. Why are you exercising authority against us in the mountains?
text.NRSV: ‘You are the only one to rise up against us, and I have fallen into ridicule and disgrace because of you. Why do you assume authority against us in the hill country?

text.DRB: Now therefore if thou trust in thy forces, come down to us in the plain, let us compare there one with another: because with me is the force of battles.
text.KJV: Now therefore, if thou trustest in thine own strength, come down to us into the plain field, and there let us try the matter together: for with me is the power of the cities.
text.NABRE: If you have confidence in your forces, come down now to us in the plain, and let us test each other’s strength there; for the forces of the cities are on my side.
text.NRSV: If you now have confidence in your forces, come down to the plain to meet us, and let us match strength with each other there, for I have with me the power of the cities.

text.DRB: Ask, & learn who I am, & the rest, that aid me, who also say that your foot cannot stand before our face, because twice have thy fathers been put to flight in their own land:
text.KJV: Ask and learn who I am, and the rest that take our part, and they shall tell thee that thy foot is not able to to flight in their own land.
text.NABRE: Inquire and find out who I am and who the others are who are helping me. People are saying that you cannot make a stand against us because your ancestors were twice put to flight in their own land.
text.NRSV: Ask and learn who I am and who the others are that are helping us. People will tell you that you cannot stand before us, for your ancestors were twice put to flight in their own land.

text.DRB: and now how wilt thou be able to abide the horsemen, & so great an army in the plain, where is no stone, nor rock, nor place to fly?
text.KJV: Wherefore now thou shalt not be able to abide the horsemen and so great a power in the plain, where is neither stone nor flint, nor place to flee unto.
text.NABRE: Now you too will be unable to withstand our cavalry and such a force as this in the plain, where there is not a stone or a pebble or a place to flee.”
text.NRSV: And now you will not be able to withstand my cavalry and such an army in the plain, where there is no stone or pebble, or place to flee.’

text.DRB: But as Jonathas heard the words of Apollonius, he was moved in mind: & he chose ten thousand men, & went forth from Jerusalem, & Simon his brother met him to aid him.
text.KJV: So when Jonathan heard these words of Apollonius, he was moved in his mind, and choosing ten thousand men he went out of Jerusalem, where Simon his brother met him for to help him.
text.NABRE: When Jonathan heard the message of Apollonius, he was provoked. Choosing ten thousand men, he set out from Jerusalem, and Simon his brother joined him to help him.
text.NRSV: When Jonathan heard the words of Apollonius, his spirit was aroused. He chose ten thousand men and set out from Jerusalem, and his brother Simon met him to help him.

text.DRB: And they moved the camp to Joppe, & it shut him out of the city: because the garrison of Apollonius was in Joppe, & he assaulted it.
text.KJV: And he pitched his tents against Joppa: but; they of Joppa shut him out of the city, because Apollonius had a garrison there.
text.NABRE: He encamped near Joppa, but the people of the city shut him out because Apollonius had a garrison in Joppa. When they attacked it,
text.NRSV: He encamped before Joppa, but the people of the city closed its gates, for Apollonius had a garrison in Joppa.

text.DRB: And they that were in the city being put in great fear, opened to him, & Jonathas obtained Joppe.
text.KJV: Then Jonathan laid siege unto it: whereupon they of the city let him in for fear: and so Jonathan won Joppa.
text.NABRE: the people of the city became afraid and opened the gates, and so Jonathan took possession of Joppa.
text.NRSV: So they fought against it, and the people of the city became afraid and opened the gates, and Jonathan gained possession of Joppa.

text.DRB: And Apollonius heard it, & he took three thousand horsemen, & a great army.
text.KJV: Whereof when Apollonius heard, he took three thousand horsemen, with a great host of footmen, and went to Azotus as one that journeyed, and therewithal drew him forth into the plain. because he had a great number of horsemen, in whom he put his trust.
text.NABRE: When Apollonius heard of it, he drew up three thousand cavalry and a large force of infantry. He marched toward Azotus as though he were going on through, but at the same time he was advancing into the plain, because he had such a large number of cavalry to rely on.
text.NRSV: When Apollonius heard of it, he mustered three thousand cavalry and a large army, and went to Azotus as though he were going farther. At the same time he advanced into the plain, for he had a large troop of cavalry and put confidence in it.

text.DRB: And he went to Azotus as making journey, and immediately he went forth into the plain: because he had a multitude of horsemen, and he trusted in them. And Jonathas pursued him unto Azotus, and they joined battle.
text.KJV: Then Jonathan followed after him to Azotus, where the armies joined battle.
text.NABRE: Jonathan pursued him toward Azotus, and the armies engaged in battle.
text.NRSV: Jonathan pursued him to Azotus, and the armies engaged in battle.

text.DRB: And Apollonius left in the camp a thousand horsemen behind them secretly.
text.KJV: Now Apollonius had left a thousand horsemen in ambush.
text.NABRE: Apollonius, however, had left a thousand cavalry in hiding behind them.
text.NRSV: Now Apollonius had secretly left a thousand cavalry behind them.

text.DRB: And Jonathas understood that there was an embushment behind him, and they compassed his camp, and they threw darts upon the people from morning even unto evening.
text.KJV: And Jonathan knew that there was an ambushment behind him; for they had compassed in his host, and cast darts at the people, from morning till evening.
text.NABRE: Jonathan discovered that there was an ambush behind him; his army was surrounded. From morning until evening they showered his troops with arrows.
text.NRSV: Jonathan learned that there was an ambush behind him, for they surrounded his army and shot arrows at his men from early morning until late afternoon.

text.DRB: But the people stood, as Jonathas had commanded: and their horses laboured.
text.KJV: But the people stood still, as Jonathan had commanded them: and so the enemies’ horses were tired.
text.NABRE: But his troops held their ground, as Jonathan had commanded, while the enemy’s horses became tired out.
text.NRSV: But his men stood fast, as Jonathan had commanded, and the enemy’s horses grew tired.

text.DRB: And Simon put forth his army, and joined it against the legion: for the horsemen were wearied: and they were discomfited by him, and fled.
text.KJV: Then brought Simon forth his host, and set them against the footmen, (for the horsemen were spent) who were discomfited by him, and fled.
text.NABRE: Then Simon brought forward his force, and engaged the phalanx in battle. Since the cavalry were exhausted, the phalanx was crushed by him and fled,
text.NRSV: Then Simon brought forward his force and engaged the phalanx in battle (for the cavalry was exhausted); they were overwhelmed by him and fled,

text.DRB: And they that were scattered by the plain, fled into Azotus, and entered into Bethdagon their idol house, there to save themselves.
text.KJV: The horsemen also, being scattered in the field, fled to Azotus, and went into Bethdagon, their idol’s temple, for safety.
text.NABRE: while the cavalry too were scattered over the plain. They fled to Azotus and entered Beth-dagon, the temple of their idol, to save themselves.
text.NRSV: and the cavalry was dispersed in the plain. They fled to Azotus and entered Beth-dagon, the temple of their idol, for safety.

text.DRB: And Jonathas burnt Azotus, and the cities that were round about it, and he took the spoils of them, and the temple of Dagon: and all that fled into it, he burnt with fire.
text.KJV: But Jonathan set fire on Azotus, and the cities round about it, and took their spoils; and the temple of Dagon, with them that were fled into it, he burned with fire.
text.NABRE: But Jonathan burned and plundered Azotus with its neighboring towns, and destroyed by fire both the temple of Dagon and those who had taken refuge in it.
text.NRSV: But Jonathan burned Azotus and the surrounding towns and plundered them; and the temple of Dagon, and those who had taken refuge in it, he burned with fire.

text.DRB: And there were that were slain by the sword, with these that were burnt, almost eight thousand men.
text.KJV: Thus there were burned and slain with the sword well nigh eight thousand men.
text.NABRE: Those who fell by the sword, together with those who were burned alive, came to about eight thousand.
text.NRSV: The number of those who fell by the sword, with those burned alive, came to eight thousand.

text.DRB: And Jonathas removed the camp from thence, and brought it to Ascalon: and they went out of the city to meet him in great glory.
text.KJV: And from thence Jonathan removed his host, and camped against Ascalon, where the men of the city came forth, and met him with great pomp.
text.NABRE: Then Jonathan left there and encamped at Askalon, and the people of that city came out to meet him with great pomp.
text.NRSV: Then Jonathan left there and encamped against Askalon, and the people of the city came out to meet him with great pomp.

text.DRB: And Jonathas returned into Jerusalem with his company, having many spoils.
text.KJV: After this returned Jonathan and his host unto Jerusalem, having any spoils.
text.NABRE: Jonathan and those with him then returned to Jerusalem, with much spoil.
text.NRSV: He and those with him then returned to Jerusalem with a large amount of booty.

text.DRB: And it came to pass: as Alexander the king heard these words, he added yet to glorify Jonathas.
text.KJV: Now when king Alexander heard these things, he honoured Jonathan yet more.
text.NABRE: When King Alexander heard of these events, he accorded new honors to Jonathan.
text.NRSV: When King Alexander heard of these things, he honoured Jonathan still more;

text.DRB: And he sent him a chain of gold, as the custom is to be given to the cousins of kings. And he gave him Accaron and all the borders thereof in possession.
text.KJV: And sent him a buckle of gold, as the use is to be given to such as are of the king’s blood: he gave him also Accaron with the borders thereof in possession.
text.NABRE: He sent him a gold buckle, such as is usually given to King’s Kinsmen; he also gave him Ekron and all its territory as a possession.
text.NRSV: and he sent to him a golden buckle, such as it is the custom to give to the King’s Kinsmen. He also gave him Ekron and all its environs as his possession.

text.KJV: And the king of Egypt gathered together a great host, like the sand that lieth upon the sea shore, and many ships, and went about through deceit to get Alexander’s kingdom, and join it to his own.
text.NABRE: Chapter 11 Alliance of Ptolemy and Demetrius II. Then the king of Egypt gathered forces as numerous as the sands of the seashore, and many ships; and he sought by deceit to take Alexander’s kingdom and add it to his own.
text.NRSV: Ptolemy Invades Syria 11
text.DRB: And the king of Ægypt gathered an army, as the sand that is about the sea shore, and many ships: and he sought to win the kingdom of Alexander by guile, and to add it to his own kingdom.

And he went out into Syria with peaceable words, & they opened to him the cities, and met him: because Alexander the king had commanded them to go forth to meet him, for that he was his father in law.
text.KJV: Whereupon he took his journey into Spain in peaceable manner, so as they of the cities opened unto him, and met him: for king Alexander had commanded them so to do, because he was his brother in law.
text.NABRE: He set out for Syria with peaceful words, and the people in the cities opened their gates to welcome him, as King Alexander had ordered them to do, since Ptolemy was his father-in-law.
text.NRSV: He set out for Syria with peaceable words, and the people of the towns opened their gates to him and went to meet him, for King Alexander had commanded them to meet him, since he was Alexander’s father-in-law.

text.DRB: But when Ptolomee entered the cities, he put garrisons of soldiers in every city.
text.KJV: Now as Ptolemee entered into the cities, he set in every one of them a garrison of soldiers to keep it.
text.NABRE: But when Ptolemy entered the cities, he stationed a garrison of troops in each one.
text.NRSV: But when Ptolemy entered the towns he stationed forces as a garrison in each town.

text.DRB: And as he approached to Azotus, they shewed him the temple of Dagon burnt with fire, and Azotus, & the rest thereof thrown down, and the bodies cast forth, & the graves of them that were slain in the battle, which they had made near the way.
text.KJV: And when he came near to Azotus, they shewed him the temple of Dagon that was burnt, and Azotus and the suburbs thereof that were destroyed, and the bodies that were cast abroad and them that he had burnt in the battle; for they had made heaps of them by the way where he should pass.
text.NABRE: As they neared Azotus, they showed him the temple of Dagon destroyed by fire, Azotus and its suburbs demolished, corpses lying about, and the charred bodies of those burned in the war, for they had heaped them up along his route.
text.NRSV: When he approached Azotus, they showed him the burnt-out temple of Dagon, and Azotus and its suburbs destroyed, and the corpses lying about, and the charred bodies of those whom Jonathan had burned in the war, for they had piled them in heaps along his route.

text.DRB: And they told the king that Jonathas did these things: to raise envy against him: and the king held his peace.
text.KJV: Also they told the king whatsoever Jonathan had done, to the intent he might blame him: but the king held his peace.
text.NABRE: They told the king what Jonathan had done in order to denigrate him; but the king said nothing.
text.NRSV: They also told the king what Jonathan had done, to throw blame on him; but the king kept silent.

text.DRB: And Jonathas came to meet the king into Joppe with glory, and they saluted one another, and they slept there.
text.KJV: Then Jonathan met the king with great pomp at Joppa, where they saluted one another, and lodged.
text.NABRE: Jonathan met the king with pomp at Joppa, and they greeted each other and spent the night there.
text.NRSV: Jonathan met the king at Joppa with pomp, and they greeted one another and spent the night there.

text.DRB: And Jonathas went with the king even to the river, that was called Eleutherus, and he returned into Jerusalem.
text.KJV: Afterward Jonathan, when he had gone with the king to the river called Eleutherus, returned again to Jerusalem.
text.NABRE: Jonathan accompanied the king as far as the river called Eleutherus and then returned to Jerusalem.
text.NRSV: And Jonathan went with the king as far as the river called Eleutherus; then he returned to Jerusalem.

text.DRB: And king Ptolomee obtained the dominion of the cities even to Seleucia by the sea side, and he purposed evil purposes against Alexander.
text.KJV: King Ptolemee therefore, having gotten the dominion of the cities by the sea unto Seleucia upon the sea coast, imagined wicked counsels against Alexander.
text.NABRE: And so King Ptolemy took possession of the cities along the seacoast as far as Seleucia by the sea, plotting evil schemes against Alexander all the while.
text.NRSV: So King Ptolemy gained control of the coastal cities as far as Seleucia by the sea, and he kept devising wicked designs against Alexander.

text.DRB: And sent legates to Demetrius, saying: Come, let us make a league between us, and I will give thee my daughter that Alexander hath, and thou shalt reign in the kingdom of thy father.
text.KJV: Whereupon he sent ambassadors unto king Demetrius, saying, Come, let us make a league betwixt us, and I will give thee my daughter whom Alexander hath, and thou shalt reign in thy father’s kingdom:
text.NABRE: He sent ambassadors to King Demetrius, saying: “Come, let us make a covenant with each other; I will give you my daughter whom Alexander has married, and you shall reign over your father’s kingdom.
text.NRSV: He sent envoys to King Demetrius, saying, ‘Come, let us make a covenant with each other, and I will give you in marriage my daughter who was Alexander’s wife, and you shall reign over your father’s kingdom.

text.DRB: For it repenteth me that I have given him my daughter: for he hath sought to kill me.
text.KJV: For I repent that I gave my daughter unto him, for he sought to slay me.
text.NABRE: I regret that I gave him my daughter, for he has sought to kill me.”
text.NRSV: I now regret that I gave him my daughter, for he has tried to kill me.’

text.DRB: And he dispraised him, for that he coveted his kingdom,
text.KJV: Thus did he slander him, because he was desirous of his kingdom.
text.NABRE: He was criticizing Alexander, however, because he coveted his kingdom.
text.NRSV: He threw blame on Alexander because he coveted his kingdom.

text.DRB: and he took away his daughter, & gave her to Demetrius, and alienated himself from Alexander, and his enmities were made manifest.
text.KJV: Wherefore he took his daughter from him, and gave her to Demetrius, and forsook Alexander, so that their hatred was openly known.
text.NABRE: After taking his daughter away, Ptolemy gave her to Demetrius and broke with Alexander; the enmity between them was now evident.
text.NRSV: So he took his daughter away from him and gave her to Demetrius. He was estranged from Alexander, and their enmity became manifest.

text.KJV: Then Ptolemee entered into Antioch, where he set two crowns upon his head, the crown of Asia, and of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Then Ptolemy entered Antioch and assumed the crown of Asia; thus he set upon his head two crowns, that of Egypt and that of Asia.
text.NRSV: Then Ptolemy entered Antioch and put on the crown of Asia. Thus he put two crowns on his head, the crown of Egypt and that of Asia.
text.DRB: And Ptolomee entered into Antioch, & he put two crowns upon his head, of Ægypt, & of Asia.
But Alexander the king was in Cilicia at that time: because they rebelled that were in those places.
text.KJV: In the mean season was king Alexander in Cilicia, because those that dwelt in those parts had revolted from him.
text.NABRE: Now King Alexander was in Cilicia at that time, because the people of that region had revolted.
text.NRSV: Now King Alexander was in Cilicia at that time, because the people of that region were in revolt.

text.DRB: And Alexander heard it, and came to him into battle: and Ptolomee the king brought forth an army, and met him with a strong power, and put him to flight.
text.KJV: But when Alexander heard of this, he came to war against him: whereupon king Ptolemee brought forth his host, and met him with a mighty power, and put him to flight.
text.NABRE: When Alexander heard the news, he came against Ptolemy in battle. Ptolemy marched out and met him with a strong force and routed him.
text.NRSV: When Alexander heard of it, he came against him in battle. Ptolemy marched out and met him with a strong force, and put him to flight.

text.KJV: So Alexander fled into Arabia there to be defended; but king Ptolemee was exalted:
text.NABRE: When Alexander fled to Arabia to seek protection, King Ptolemy was triumphant.
text.NRSV: So Alexander fled into Arabia to find protection there, and King Ptolemy was triumphant.
text.DRB: And Alexander fled into Arabia, there to be protected: and king Ptolomee was exalted.
And Zabdiel an Arabian took off Alexander's head, & sent it to Ptolomee.
text.KJV: For Zabdiel the Arabian took off Alexander’s head, and sent it unto Ptolemee.
text.NABRE: Zabdiel the Arabian cut off Alexander’s head and sent it to Ptolemy.
text.NRSV: Zabdiel the Arab cut off the head of Alexander and sent it to Ptolemy.

text.DRB: And king Ptolomee died the third day: & they that were in the fortresses, perished by them that were within the camp.
text.KJV: King Ptolemee also died the third day after, and they that were in the strong holds were slain one of another.
text.NABRE: But three days later King Ptolemy himself died, and his troops in the strongholds were killed by the inhabitants of the strongholds.
text.NRSV: But King Ptolemy died three days later, and his troops in the strongholds were killed by the inhabitants of the strongholds.

text.DRB: And Demetrius reigned the year an hundreth sixty seven.
text.KJV: By this means Demetrius reigned in the hundred threescore and seventh year.
text.NABRE: Thus Demetrius became king in the one hundred and sixty-seventh year.
text.NRSV: So Demetrius became king in the one hundred and sixty-seventh year.

text.DRB: In those days Jonathas gathered together them, that were in Jurie, to win the castle in Jerusalem: and they made engines against it.
text.KJV: At the same time Jonathan gathered together them that were in Judea to take the tower that was in Jerusalem: and he made many engines of war against it.
text.NABRE: Alliance of Jonathan and Demetrius II. In those days Jonathan gathered together the people of Judea to attack the citadel in Jerusalem, and they set up many siege engines against it.
text.NRSV: Jonathan’s Diplomacy In those days Jonathan assembled the Judeans to attack the citadel in Jerusalem, and he built many engines of war to use against it.

text.DRB: And certain that hated their own nation wicked men, went to king Demetrius, and told him that Jonathas besieged the castle.
text.KJV: Then came ungodly persons, who hated their own people, went unto the king, and told him that Jonathan besieged the tower,
text.NABRE: But some transgressors of the law, enemies of their own nation, went to the king and informed him that Jonathan was besieging the citadel.
text.NRSV: But certain renegades who hated their nation went to the king and reported to him that Jonathan was besieging the citadel.

text.DRB: And as he heard it, he was wrath: and forthwith he came to Ptolemais, and wrote to Jonathas, that he should not besiege the castle, but should meet him to talk together in haste.
text.KJV: Whereof when he heard, he was angry, and immediately removing, he came to Ptolemais, and wrote unto Jonathan, that he should not lay siege to the tower, but come and speak with him at Ptolemais in great haste.
text.NABRE: When Demetrius heard this, he was enraged; and as soon as he heard it, he set out and came to Ptolemais. He wrote to Jonathan to discontinue the siege and to meet him for a conference at Ptolemais as soon as possible.
text.NRSV: When he heard this he was angry, and as soon as he heard it he set out and came to Ptolemais; and he wrote to order Jonathan not to continue the siege, but to meet him for a conference at Ptolemais as quickly as possible.

text.DRB: But as Jonathas heard it, he bade them besiege it: and he chose of the ancients of Israel, and of the Priests, and put himself in hazard.
text.KJV: Nevertheless Jonathan, when he heard this, commanded to besiege it still: and he chose certain of the elders of Israel and the priests, and put himself in peril;
text.NABRE: On hearing this, Jonathan ordered the siege to continue. He selected some elders and priests of Israel and put himself at risk.
text.NRSV: When Jonathan heard this, he gave orders to continue the siege. He chose some of the elders of Israel and some of the priests, and put himself in danger,

text.DRB: And he took gold, & silver, and raiments, & many other presents, & went to the king to Ptolomais, and he found grace in his sight.
text.KJV: And took silver and gold, and raiment, and divers presents besides, and went to Ptolemais unto the king, where he found favour in his sight.
text.NABRE: Taking with him silver, gold and apparel, and many other presents, he went to the king at Ptolemais, and found favor with him.
text.NRSV: for he went to the king at Ptolemais, taking silver and gold and clothing and numerous other gifts. And he won his favour.

text.DRB: And certain wicked men of his nation solicited against him.
text.KJV: And though certain ungodly men of the people had made complaints against him,
text.NABRE: Although certain renegades of his own nation kept on bringing charges against him,
text.NRSV: Although certain renegades of his nation kept making complaints against him,

text.DRB: And the king did to him, as they had done to him which had been before him: and he exalted him in the sight of all his friends,
text.KJV: Yet the king entreated him as his predecessors had done before, and promoted him in the sight of all his friends,
text.NABRE: the king treated him just as his predecessors had done and exalted him in the presence of all his Friends.
text.NRSV: the king treated him as his predecessors had treated him; he exalted him in the presence of all his Friends.

text.DRB: and he established to him the chief Priesthood, & whatsoever other things he had before precious, & he made him the chief of his friends.
text.KJV: And confirmed him in the high priesthood, and in all the honours that he had before, and gave him preeminence among his chief friends.
text.NABRE: He confirmed him in the high priesthood and in the other honors he had previously held, and had him enrolled among his Chief Friends.
text.NRSV: He confirmed him in the high-priesthood and in as many other honours as he had formerly had, and caused him to be reckoned among his chief Friends.

text.DRB: And Jonathas requested of the king that he would make Jury free, & the three toparchies, and Samaria, & the confines thereof: and he promised him three hundred talents.
text.KJV: Then Jonathan desired the king, that he would make Judea free from tribute, as also the three governments, with the country of Samaria; and he promised him three hundred talents.
text.NABRE: Jonathan asked the king to exempt Judea and the three districts of Samaria from tribute, promising him in return three hundred talents.
text.NRSV: Then Jonathan asked the king to free Judea and the three districts of Samaria from tribute, and promised him three hundred talents.

text.DRB: And the king consented: and he wrote to Jonathas epistles of all these things, containing this tenure.
text.KJV: So the king consented, and wrote letters unto Jonathan of all these things after this manner:
text.NABRE: The king agreed and wrote a letter to Jonathan about all these matters as follows:
text.NRSV: The king consented, and wrote a letter to Jonathan about all these things; its contents were as follows:

text.DRB: King Demetrius to his brother Jonathas greeting, and to the nation of the Jews.
text.KJV: King Demetrius unto his brother Jonathan, and unto the nation of the Jews, sendeth greeting:
text.NABRE: “King Demetrius sends greetings to his brother Jonathan and to the Jewish nation.
text.NRSV: ‘King Demetrius to his brother Jonathan and to the nation of the Jews, greetings.

text.DRB: Acopy of the epistle, which we have written to Lasthenes our parent concerning you, we have sent to you that you might know it.
text.KJV: We send you here a copy of the letter which we did write unto our cousin Lasthenes concerning you, that ye might see it.
text.NABRE: We are sending you, for your information, a copy of the letter that we wrote to Lasthenes our Kinsman concerning you.
text.NRSV: This copy of the letter that we wrote concerning you to our kinsman Lasthenes we have written to you also, so that you may know what it says.

text.DRB: King Demetrius to Lasthenes his parent greeting.
text.KJV: King Demetrius unto his father Lasthenes sendeth greeting:
text.NABRE: ‘King Demetrius sends greetings to his father Lasthenes.
text.NRSV: “King Demetrius to his father Lasthenes, greetings.

text.DRB: We have determined to do good to the nation of the Jews our friends, & that keep the things that are just with us, for their gentleness which they bear towards us.
text.KJV: We are determined to do good to the people of the Jews, who are our friends, and keep covenants with us, because of their good will toward us.
text.NABRE: Upon the Jewish nation, who are our friends and observe their obligations to us, we have decided to bestow benefits because of the good will they show us.
text.NRSV: We have determined to do good to the nation of the Jews, who are our friends and fulfil their obligations to us, because of the goodwill they show towards us.

text.DRB: We have ordained therefore unto them all the coasts of Jurie, & the three cities, Apherema, Lyda, & Ramatha, which are added to Jurie of Samaria, & all their confines, to be sequestered to all them that sacrifice in Jerusalem, for these things, which the king received of them every year, and for the fruits of the land, and of the trees.
text.KJV: Wherefore we have ratified unto them the borders of Judea, with the three governments of Apherema and Lydda and Ramathem, that are added unto Judea from the country of Samaria, and all things appertaining unto them, for all such as do sacrifice in Jerusalem, instead of the payments which the king received of them yearly aforetime out of the fruits of the earth and of trees.
text.NABRE: Therefore we confirm their possession, not only of the territory of Judea, but also of the three districts of Aphairema, Lydda, and Ramathaim. These districts, together with all their dependencies, are hereby transferred from Samaria to Judea for those who offer sacrifices in Jerusalem in lieu of the royal taxes the king used to receive yearly from the produce of earth and trees.
text.NRSV: We have confirmed as their possession both the territory of Judea and the three districts of Aphairema and Lydda and Rathamin; the latter, with all the region bordering them, were added to Judea from Samaria. To all those who offer sacrifice in Jerusalem we have granted release from the royal taxes that the king formerly received from them each year, from the crops of the land and the fruit of the trees.

text.DRB: And other things, that pertained to us of the tithes, and of the tributes, from this time we remit to them: & the salt floors, and the crowns, that were presented to us,
text.KJV: And as for other things that belong unto us, of the tithes and customs pertaining unto us, as also the saltpits, and the crown taxes, which are due unto us, we discharge them of them all for their relief.
text.NABRE: From payment of the other things that would henceforth be due to us, namely, the tithes and taxes, as well as the salt tax, and the crown tax—from all these we grant them release.
text.NRSV: And the other payments henceforth due to us of the tithes, and the taxes due to us, and the salt pits and the crown taxes due to us—from all these we shall grant them release.

text.DRB: we grant all to them, & nothing of these shall be broken from this time, and for ever.
text.KJV: And nothing hereof shall be revoked from this time forth for ever.
text.NABRE: Henceforth and forever not one of these provisions shall ever be revoked.
text.NRSV: And not one of these grants shall be cancelled from this time on for ever.

text.DRB: Now therefore provide to make a copy of these, and let it be given to Jonathas, & let it be laid in the holy mount, and in a solemn place.
text.KJV: Now therefore see that thou make a copy of these things, and let it be delivered unto Jonathan, and set upon the holy mount in a conspicuous place.
text.NABRE: See to it, therefore, that a copy of these instructions be made and given to Jonathan. Let it be displayed on the holy mountain in a conspicuous place.’”
text.NRSV: Now therefore take care to make a copy of this, and let it be given to Jonathan and put up in a conspicuous place on the holy mountain.”’

text.DRB: And Demetrius the king seeing that the land was quiet in his sight, & nothing resisted him, he dismissed all his army, every man to his place, except the foreign army, which he brought from the isles of the Gentiles: and all the bands of his fathers were enemies to him.
text.KJV: After this, when king Demetrius saw that the land was quiet before him, and that no resistance was made against him, he sent away all his forces, every one to his own place, except certain bands of strangers, whom he had gathered from the isles of the heathen: wherefore all the forces of his fathers hated him.
text.NABRE: The Intrigue of Trypho. When King Demetrius saw that the land was peaceful under his rule and that he had no opposition, he dismissed his entire army, each to his own home, except the foreign troops which he had hired from the islands of the nations. So all the soldiers who had served under his predecessors became hostile to him.
text.NRSV: The Intrigue of Trypho When King Demetrius saw that the land was quiet before him and that there was no opposition to him, he dismissed all his troops, all of them to their own homes, except the foreign troops that he had recruited from the islands of the nations. So all the troops who had served under his predecessors hated him.

text.DRB: And there was one Tryphon of Alexander's part before: and he saw that all the army murmured against Demetrius, & he went to Emalchuel the Arabian, that brought up Antiochus the son of Alexander:
text.KJV: Moreover there was one Tryphon, that had been of Alexander’s part afore, who, seeing that all the host murmured against Demetrius, went to Simalcue the Arabian that brought up Antiochus the young son of Alexander,
text.NABRE: When a certain Trypho, who had previously supported Alexander, saw that all the troops were grumbling against Demetrius, he went to Imalkue the Arabian, who was raising Alexander’s young son Antiochus.
text.NRSV: A certain Trypho had formerly been one of Alexander’s supporters; he saw that all the troops were grumbling against Demetrius. So he went to Imalkue the Arab, who was bringing up Antiochus, the young son of Alexander,

text.DRB: And he lay upon him, to deliver this Antiochus unto him, to reign in his father's place: and he declared unto him what great things Demetrius did, and the enmities of his hosts against him. And he tarried there many days.
text.KJV: And lay sore upon him to deliver him this young Antiochus, that he might reign in his father’s stead: he told him therefore all that Demetrius had done, and how his men of war were at enmity with him, and there he remained a long season.
text.NABRE: Trypho kept urging Imalkue to hand over the boy to him, so that he might succeed his father as king. He told him of all that Demetrius had done and of the hostility his soldiers had for him; and he remained there for many days.
text.NRSV: and insistently urged him to hand Antiochus over to him, to become king in place of his father. He also reported to Imalkue what Demetrius had done and told of the hatred that the troops of Demetrius had for him; and he stayed there for many days.

text.DRB: And Jonathas sent to Demetrius the king, that he would cast out them, that were in the castle in Jerusalem, and that were in the holds: because they impugned Israel.
text.KJV: In the mean time Jonathan sent unto king Demetrius, that he would cast those of the tower out of Jerusalem, and those also in the fortresses: for they fought against Israel.
text.NABRE: Jonathan Aids Demetrius II. Meanwhile Jonathan sent the request to King Demetrius to withdraw the troops in the citadel from Jerusalem and from the other strongholds, for they were constantly waging war on Israel.
text.NRSV: Now Jonathan sent to King Demetrius the request that he remove the troops of the citadel from Jerusalem, and the troops in the strongholds; for they kept fighting against Israel.

text.DRB: And Demetrius sent to Jonathas, saying: I will not only do these things for thee, & for thy nation, but with glory I will honour thee, & thy nation, when it shall be time convenient.
text.KJV: So Demetrius sent unto Jonathan, saying, I will not only do this for thee and thy people, but I will greatly honour thee and thy nation, if opportunity serve.
text.NABRE: Demetrius, in turn, sent this word to Jonathan: “I will do not only this for you and your nation, but I will greatly honor you and your nation when I find the opportunity.
text.NRSV: And Demetrius sent this message back to Jonathan: ‘Not only will I do these things for you and your nation, but I will confer great honour on you and your nation, if I find an opportunity.

text.DRB: Now therefore thou shalt do well if thou wilt send men to help me: because all mine army is departed.
text.KJV: Now therefore thou shalt do well, if thou send me men to help me; for all my forces are gone from me.
text.NABRE: Now, therefore, you will do well to send men to fight for me, because all my troops have revolted.”
text.NRSV: Now then, you will do well to send me men who will help me, for all my troops have revolted.’

text.DRB: And Jonathas sent him three thousand valiant men to Antioch: and they came to the king, & the king was delighted at their coming.
text.KJV: Upon this Jonathan sent him three thousand strong men unto Antioch: and when they came to the king, the king was very glad of their coming.
text.NABRE: So Jonathan sent three thousand good fighting men to him at Antioch. When they came to the king, he was delighted over their arrival.
text.NRSV: So Jonathan sent three thousand stalwart men to him at Antioch, and when they came to the king, the king rejoiced at their arrival.

text.DRB: And there assembled they that were of the city, an hundred twenty thousand men, and they would have slain the king.
text.KJV: Howbeit they that were of the city gathered themselves together into the midst of the city, to the number of an hundred and twenty thousand men, and would have slain the king.
text.NABRE: The populace, one hundred and twenty thousand strong, massed in the center of the city in an attempt to kill the king.
text.NRSV: Then the people of the city assembled within the city, to the number of a hundred and twenty thousand, and they wanted to kill the king.

text.DRB: And the king fled into the court: & they that were of the city kept the ways of the city, and began to fight.
text.KJV: Wherefore the king fled into the court, but they of the city kept the passages of the city, and began to fight.
text.NABRE: So the king took refuge in the palace, while the populace gained control of the main streets of the city and prepared for battle.
text.NRSV: But the king fled into the palace. Then the people of the city seized the main streets of the city and began to fight.

text.DRB: And the king called the Jews for aid: & they came together to him all at once, & they were all dispersed through the city.
text.KJV: Then the king called to the Jews for help, who came unto him all at once, and dispersing themselves through the city slew that day in the city to the number of an hundred thousand.
text.NABRE: Then the king called the Jewish force to his aid. They all rallied around him and spread out through the city. On that day they killed about a hundred thousand in the city.
text.NRSV: So the king called the Jews to his aid, and they all rallied around him and then spread out through the city; and they killed on that day about one hundred thousand.

text.DRB: And they slew in that day an hundred thousand men, & they burnt the city, & took many spoils in that day, & delivered the king.
text.KJV: Also they set fire on the city, and gat many spoils that day, and delivered the king.
text.NABRE: At the same time, they set the city on fire and took much spoil. Thus they saved the king.
text.NRSV: They set fire to the city and seized a large amount of spoil on that day, and saved the king.

text.DRB: And they that were of the city saw, that the Jews had obtained the city as they would: and they were discomfited in their mind, and cried to the king with petitions, saying:
text.KJV: So when they of the city saw that the Jews had got the city as they would, their courage was abated: wherefore they made supplication to the king, and cried, saying,
text.NABRE: When the populace saw that the Jewish force controlled the city, they lost courage and cried out to the king in supplication,
text.NRSV: When the people of the city saw that the Jews had gained control of the city as they pleased, their courage failed and they cried out to the king with this entreaty:

text.DRB: Give us the right hand, and let the Jews cease to oppugn us & the city.
text.KJV: Grant us peace, and let the Jews cease from assaulting us and the city.
text.NABRE: “Extend the hand of friendship to us, and make the Jews stop attacking us and the city.”
text.NRSV: ‘Grant us peace, and make the Jews stop fighting against us and our city.’

text.DRB: And they threw away their weapons, & made peace, & the Jews were glorified in the king's sight, & were renowned in the sight of all that were in his kingdom, & returned into Jerusalem with many spoils.
text.KJV: With that they cast away their weapons, and made peace; and the Jews were honoured in the sight of the king, and in the sight of all that were in his realm; and they returned to Jerusalem, having great spoils.
text.NABRE: So they threw down their weapons and made peace. The Jews thus gained honor in the eyes of the king and all his subjects, and they became renowned throughout his kingdom. Finally they returned to Jerusalem with much plunder.
text.NRSV: And they threw down their arms and made peace. So the Jews gained glory in the sight of the king and of all the people in his kingdom, and they returned to Jerusalem with a large amount of spoil.

text.DRB: And king Demetrius sat in the seat of his kingdom: and the land was at rest in his sight.
text.KJV: So king Demetrius sat on the throne of his kingdom, and the land was quiet before him.
text.NABRE: But when King Demetrius was sure of his royal throne, and the land was peaceful under his rule,
text.NRSV: So King Demetrius sat on the throne of his kingdom, and the land was quiet before him.

text.DRB: And he feigned all things whatsoever he said, & alienated himself from Jonathas, and he requited him not according to the benefits, that he had given him, and he vexed him exceedingly.
text.KJV: Nevertheless he dissembled in all that ever he spake, and estranged himself from Jonathan, neither rewarded he him according to the benefits which he had received of him, but troubled him very sore.
text.NABRE: he broke all his promises and became estranged from Jonathan. Instead of repaying Jonathan for all the favors he had received from him, he caused him much distress.
text.NRSV: But he broke his word about all that he had promised; he became estranged from Jonathan and did not repay the favours that Jonathan had done him, but treated him very harshly.

text.DRB: And after these things Tryphon returned, & with him Antiochus the young boy, who reigned, and put the crown upon him.
text.KJV: After this returned Tryphon, and with him the young child Antiochus, who reigned, and was crowned.
text.NABRE: Alliance of Jonathan and Antiochus VI. After this, Trypho returned and brought with him the young boy Antiochus, who became king and put on the diadem.
text.NRSV: Trypho Seizes Power After this Trypho returned, and with him the young boy Antiochus who began to reign and put on the crown.

text.DRB: And there gathered unto him all the bands, which Demetrius had dispersed, and they fought against him: & he fled, and turned the back.
text.KJV: Then there gathered unto him all the men of war, whom Demetrius had put away, and they fought against Demetrius, who turned his back and fled.
text.NABRE: All the soldiers whom Demetrius had discharged rallied around Antiochus and fought against Demetrius, who was routed and fled.
text.NRSV: All the troops that Demetrius had discharged gathered around him; they fought against Demetrius, and he fled and was routed.

text.DRB: And Tryphon took the beasts, and won Antioch.
text.KJV: Moreover Tryphon took the elephants, and won Antioch.
text.NABRE: Trypho captured the elephants and occupied Antioch.
text.NRSV: Trypho captured the elephants and gained control of Antioch.

text.KJV: At that time young Antiochus wrote unto Jonathan, saying, I confirm thee in the high priesthood, and appoint thee ruler over the four governments, and to be one of the king’s friends.
text.NABRE: Then young Antiochus wrote to Jonathan: “I confirm you in the high priesthood and appoint you ruler over the four districts, and to be one of the King’s Friends.”
text.NRSV: Then the young Antiochus wrote to Jonathan, saying, ‘I confirm you in the high-priesthood and set you over the four districts and make you one of the king’s Friends.’
text.DRB: And Antiochus the young man wrote to Jonathas, saying: I appoint to thee the priesthood, & I place thee over the four cities, that thou mayst be of the king's friends.
And he sent him vessels of gold for his service, and he gave him leave to drink in gold, and to be in purple, & to have a chain of gold:
text.KJV: Upon this he sent him golden vessels to be served in, and gave him leave to drink in gold, and to be clothed in purple, and to wear a golden buckle.
text.NABRE: He also sent him gold dishes and a table service, gave him the right to drink from gold cups, to dress in royal purple, and to wear a gold buckle.
text.NRSV: He also sent him gold plates and a table service, and granted him the right to drink from gold cups and dress in purple and wear a gold buckle.

text.DRB: And he appointed Simon his brother governor from the borders of Tyre even to the coasts of Ægypt.
text.KJV: His brother Simon also he made captain from the place called The ladder of Tyrus unto the borders of Egypt.
text.NABRE: Likewise, he made Jonathan’s brother Simon governor of the region from the Ladder of Tyre to the borders of Egypt.
text.NRSV: He appointed Jonathan’s brother Simon governor from the Ladder of Tyre to the borders of Egypt.

text.DRB: And Jonathas went forth, & walked through the cities beyond the river: and all the band of Syria gathered unto him to help him, and he came to Ascalon, & they met him honourably out of the city.
text.KJV: Then Jonathan went forth, and passed through the cities beyond the water, and all the forces of Syria gathered themselves unto him for to help him: and when he came to Ascalon, they of the city met him honourably.
text.NABRE: Campaigns of Jonathan and Simon. Jonathan set out and traveled through the province of West-of-Euphrates and its cities, and all the forces of Syria espoused his cause as allies. When he arrived at Askalon, the citizens welcomed him with pomp.
text.NRSV: Campaigns of Jonathan and Simon Then Jonathan set out and travelled beyond the river and among the towns, and all the army of Syria gathered to him as allies. When he came to Askalon, the people of the city met him and paid him honour.

text.DRB: And he went from thence to Gaza: and they that were in Gaza shut in themselves: and he besieged it, & burnt the things that were round about the city, and he spoiled it.
text.KJV: From whence he went to Gaza, but they of Gaza shut him out; wherefore he laid siege unto it, and burned the suburbs thereof with fire, and spoiled them.
text.NABRE: But when he set out for Gaza, the people of Gaza shut him out. So he besieged it, and burned and plundered its suburbs.
text.NRSV: From there he went to Gaza, but the people of Gaza shut him out. So he besieged it and burned its suburbs with fire and plundered them.

text.DRB: And the men of Gaza besought Jonathas, and he gave them the right hand: and he took their sons for hostages, & sent them into Jerusalem: and he walked through the country even to Damascus.
text.KJV: Afterward, when they of Gaza made supplication unto Jonathan, he made peace with them, and took the sons of their chief men for hostages, and sent them to Jerusalem, and passed through the country unto Damascus.
text.NABRE: Then the people of Gaza appealed to Jonathan, and he granted them terms of peace. He took the sons of their leaders as hostages and sent them to Jerusalem. He then traveled on through the province as far as Damascus.
text.NRSV: Then the people of Gaza pleaded with Jonathan, and he made peace with them, and took the sons of their rulers as hostages and sent them to Jerusalem. And he passed through the country as far as Damascus.

text.DRB: And Jonathas heard that the princes of Demetrius prevaricated in Cades, which is in Galilee, with a great army meaning to remove him from the affairs of the kingdom:
text.KJV: Now when Jonathan heard that Demetrius’ princes were come to Cades, which is in Galilee, with a great power, purposing to remove him out of the country,
text.NABRE: Jonathan heard that the generals of Demetrius had come with a strong force to Kadesh in Galilee, intending to remove him from office.
text.NRSV: Then Jonathan heard that the officers of Demetrius had come to Kadesh in Galilee with a large army, intending to remove him from office.

text.DRB: and he went against them: but his brother Simon he left within the Province.
text.KJV: He went to meet them, and left Simon his brother in the country.
text.NABRE: So he went to meet them, leaving his brother Simon in the province.
text.NRSV: He went to meet them, but left his brother Simon in the country.

text.DRB: And Simon approached to Bethsura, and expugned it many days, and inclosed them.
text.KJV: Then Simon encamped against Bethsura and fought against it a long season, and shut it up:
text.NABRE: Simon encamped against Beth-zur, attacked it for many days, and shut in the inhabitants.
text.NRSV: Simon encamped before Beth-zur and fought against it for many days and hemmed it in.

text.DRB: And they requested of him to take the right hands, and he gave it them: and he cast them out from thence, and took the city, & placed therein a garrison.
text.KJV: But they desired to have peace with him, which he granted them, and then put them out from thence, and took the city, and set a garrison in it.
text.NABRE: They appealed to him, and he granted them terms of peace. He expelled them from the city, took possession of it, and put a garrison there.
text.NRSV: Then they asked him to grant them terms of peace, and he did so. He removed them from there, took possession of the town, and set a garrison over it.

text.DRB: And Jonathas and his camp approached to the water of Genesar, and before it was light they watched in the plain Asor.
text.KJV: As for Jonathan and his host, they pitched at the water of Gennesar, from whence betimes in the morning they gat them to the plain of Nasor.
text.NABRE: Meanwhile, Jonathan and his army pitched their camp near the waters of Gennesaret, and at daybreak they went to the plain of Hazor.
text.NRSV: Jonathan and his army encamped by the waters of Gennesaret. Early in the morning they marched to the plain of Hazor,

text.DRB: And lo the camp of the aliens met him in the plain, and they laid embushments for him in the mountains: but he went against them.
text.KJV: And, behold, the host of strangers met them in the plain, who, having laid men in ambush for him in the mountains, came themselves over against him.
text.NABRE: There the army of the foreigners met him on the plain. Having first detached an ambush in the mountains, this army mounted a frontal attack.
text.NRSV: and there in the plain the army of the foreigners met him; they had set an ambush against him in the mountains, but they themselves met him face to face.

text.DRB: And the embushments rose out of their places, & they joined battle.
text.KJV: So when they that lay in ambush rose out of their places and joined battle, all that were of Jonathan’s side fled;
text.NABRE: Then those in ambush rose out of their places and joined in the battle.
text.NRSV: Then the men in ambush emerged from their places and joined battle.

text.DRB: And all fled that were on Jonathas' part, and none was left of them, but Mathathias the son of Absolom, and Judas the son of Calphi, chief captain of the chivalry of the host.
text.KJV: Insomuch as there was not one of them left, except Mattathias the son of Absalom, and Judas the son of Calphi, the captains of the host.
text.NABRE: All of Jonathan’s men fled; no one stayed except the army commanders Mattathias, son of Absalom, and Judas, son of Chalphi.
text.NRSV: All the men with Jonathan fled; not one of them was left except Mattathias son of Absalom and Judas son of Chalphi, commanders of the forces of the army.

text.DRB: And Jonathas rent his garments, and put earth upon his head, and prayed.
text.KJV: Then Jonathan rent his clothes, and cast earth upon his head, and prayed.
text.NABRE: Jonathan tore his clothes, threw dust on his head, and prayed.
text.NRSV: Jonathan tore his clothes, put dust on his head, and prayed.

text.DRB: And Jonathas returned to them into battle, and he put them to flight, and they fought.
text.KJV: Afterwards turning again to battle, he put them to flight, and so they ran away.
text.NABRE: Then he went back to the battle and routed them, and they fled.
text.NRSV: Then he turned back to the battle against the enemy and routed them, and they fled.

text.DRB: And they of his part that fled saw, and returned to him, and pursued with him even to Cades to their camp, and they came even thither.
text.KJV: Now when his own men that were fled saw this, they turned again unto him, and with him pursued them to Cades, even unto their own tents, and there they camped.
text.NABRE: Those of his men who were running away saw it and returned to him; and with him they pursued the enemy as far as their camp in Kadesh, and there they encamped.
text.NRSV: When his men who were fleeing saw this, they returned to him and joined him in the pursuit as far as Kadesh, to their camp, and there they encamped.

text.DRB: And there fell of the aliens in that day three thousand men: and Jonathas returned into Jerusalem.
text.KJV: So there were slain of the heathen that day about three thousand men: but Jonathan returned to Jerusalem.
text.NABRE: About three thousand of the foreign troops fell on that day. Then Jonathan returned to Jerusalem.
text.NRSV: As many as three thousand of the foreigners fell that day. And Jonathan returned to Jerusalem.

text.KJV: Now when Jonathan saw that time served him, he chose certain men, and sent them to Rome, for to confirm and renew the friendship that they had with them.
text.NABRE: Chapter 12 Alliances with Rome and Sparta. When Jonathan saw that the time was right, he chose men and sent them to Rome to confirm and renew the friendship with the Romans.
text.NRSV: Alliances with Rome and Sparta 12
text.DRB: And Jonathas saw that the time helped him, he chose men & sent them to Rome, to establish and to renew amity with them:
and to the Spartiates, & to other places he sent epistles according to the same tenure.
text.KJV: He sent letters also to the Lacedemonians, and to other places, for the same purpose.
text.NABRE: He also sent letters to the Spartans and other places to the same effect.
text.NRSV: He also sent letters to the same effect to the Spartans and to other places.

text.DRB: And they went to Rome, and entered into the court, and said: Jonathas the high priest, and the nation of the Jews have sent us to renew the amity and society according as before.
text.KJV: So they went unto Rome, and entered into the senate, and said, Jonathan the high priest, and the people of the Jews, sent us unto you, to the end ye should renew the friendship, which ye had with them, and league, as in former time.
text.NABRE: After reaching Rome, the men entered the senate chamber and said, “The high priest Jonathan and the Jewish people have sent us to renew the friendship and alliance of earlier times with them.”
text.NRSV: So they went to Rome and entered the senate chamber and said, ‘The high priest Jonathan and the Jewish nation have sent us to renew the former friendship and alliance with them.’

text.DRB: And they gave them epistles unto them by place and place, to conduct them into the land of Juda with peace.
text.KJV: Upon this the Romans gave them letters unto the governors of every place that they should bring them into the land of Judea peaceably.
text.NABRE: The Romans gave them letters addressed to authorities in various places, with the request to provide them with safe conduct to the land of Judah.
text.NRSV: And the Romans gave them letters to the people in every place, asking them to provide for the envoys safe conduct to the land of Judah.

text.DRB: And this is a copy of the epistles which Jonathas wrote to the Spartiates:
text.KJV: And this is the copy of the letters which Jonathan wrote to the Lacedemonians:
text.NABRE: This is a copy of the letter that Jonathan wrote to the Spartans:
text.NRSV: This is a copy of the letter that Jonathan wrote to the Spartans:

text.DRB: Jonathas the high Priest, and the ancients of the nation, and the Priests, and the rest of the people of the Jews, to the Spartiates their brethren greeting.
text.KJV: Jonathan the high priest, and the elders of the nation, and the priests, and the other of the Jews, unto the Lacedemonians their brethren send greeting:
text.NABRE: “Jonathan the high priest, the senate of the nation, the priests, and the rest of the Jewish people send greetings to their brothers the Spartans.
text.NRSV: ‘The high priest Jonathan, the senate of the nation, the priests, and the rest of the Jewish people to their brothers the Spartans, greetings.

text.DRB: Long ago were epistles sent to Onias the high-Priest from Arius who reigned with you, that you are our brethren, as the writing containeth, that here beneath followeth.
text.KJV: There were letters sent in times past unto Onias the high priest from Darius, who reigned then among you, to signify that ye are our brethren, as the copy here underwritten doth specify.
text.NABRE: Long ago a letter was sent to the high priest Onias from Arius, who then reigned over you, stating that you are our brothers, as the attached copy shows.
text.NRSV: Already in time past a letter was sent to the high priest Onias from Arius, who was king among you, stating that you are our brothers, as the appended copy shows.

text.DRB: And Onias received the man that was sent with honour: and he received the epistles, wherein there was signification of the society and amity.
text.KJV: At which time Onias entreated the ambassador that was sent honourably, and received the letters, wherein declaration was made of the league and friendship.
text.NABRE: Onias welcomed the envoy with honor and received the letter, which spoke clearly of alliance and friendship.
text.NRSV: Onias welcomed the envoy with honour, and received the letter, which contained a clear declaration of alliance and friendship.

text.DRB: We whereas we needed none of these, having for our comfort the holy books, that are in our hands,
text.KJV: Therefore we also, albeit we need none of these things, that we have the holy books of scripture in our hands to comfort us,
text.NABRE: Though we have no need of these things, since we have for our encouragement the holy books that are in our possession,
text.NRSV: Therefore, though we have no need of these things, since we have as encouragement the holy books that are in our hands,

text.DRB: chose rather to send unto you to renew the fraternity, lest perhaps we be made aliens from you: for much time is passed, since you sent to us.
text.KJV: Have nevertheless attempted to send unto you for the renewing of brotherhood and friendship, lest we should become strangers unto you altogether: for there is a long time passed since ye sent unto us.
text.NABRE: we have ventured to send word to you for the renewal of brotherhood and friendship, lest we become strangers to you; a long time has passed since you sent your message to us.
text.NRSV: we have undertaken to send to renew our family ties and friendship with you, so that we may not become estranged from you, for considerable time has passed since you sent your letter to us.

text.DRB: We therefore at all time without intermission in the solemn days, and the rest wherein we should, are mindful of you in the sacrifices, that we offer, and in the observances, as it is meet, and becometh to remember brethren.
text.KJV: We therefore at all times without ceasing, both in our feasts, and other convenient days, do remember you in the sacrifices which we offer, and in our prayers, as reason is, and as it becometh us to think upon our brethren:
text.NABRE: We, on our part, have unceasingly remembered you in the sacrifices and prayers that we offer on our feasts and other appropriate days, as it is right and proper to remember brothers.
text.NRSV: We therefore remember you constantly on every occasion, both at our festivals and on other appropriate days, at the sacrifices that we offer and in our prayers, as it is right and proper to remember brothers.

text.DRB: We rejoice therefore of your glory.
text.KJV: And we are right glad of your honour.
text.NABRE: We likewise rejoice in your renown.
text.NRSV: And we rejoice in your glory.

text.DRB: But many tribulations have compassed us, and many battles, and the kings that are round about us, have impugned us.
text.KJV: As for ourselves, we have had great troubles and wars on every side, forsomuch as the kings that are round about us have fought against us.
text.NABRE: But many tribulations and many wars have beset us, and the kings around us have attacked us.
text.NRSV: But as for ourselves, many trials and many wars have encircled us; the kings around us have waged war against us.

text.DRB: We therefore would not be troublesome to you, nor to the rest our fellows & friends in these battles.
text.KJV: Howbeit we would not be troublesome unto you, nor to others of our confederates and friends, in these wars:
text.NABRE: We did not wish to be troublesome to you and to the rest of our allies and friends in these wars.
text.NRSV: We were unwilling to annoy you and our other allies and friends with these wars,

text.DRB: For we have had aid from heaven, and we are delivered, and our enemies are humbled.
text.KJV: For we have help from heaven that succoureth us, so as we are delivered from our enemies, and our enemies are brought under foot.
text.NABRE: For we have the help of Heaven for our support, and we have been saved from our enemies, and our enemies have been humbled.
text.NRSV: for we have the help that comes from Heaven for our aid, and so we were delivered from our enemies, and our enemies were humbled.

text.DRB: We have chosen therefore Numenius the son of Antiochus, and Antipater the son of Jason, and have sent to the Romans to renew with them the old amity and society.
text.KJV: For this cause we chose Numenius the son of Antiochus, and Antipater he son of Jason, and sent them unto the Romans, to renew the amity that we had with them, and the former league.
